items.question_text: What is the primary purpose of the ATC system?
items.explanation: The primary purpose of the ATC system is preventing collisions involving aircraft operating in the system. Additional services and expeditious flow matter, but they do not replace that primary purpose.

choices.text: Prevent collisions involving aircraft operating in the system.
Provide the most expeditious movement for every aircraft regardless of separation needs.
Provide additional services whenever pilots request them, regardless of workload.
Support National Security and Homeland Defense as the system's only purpose.

items.question_text: When several controller actions are competing, which duties receive first priority?
items.explanation: Paragraph 2-1-2 gives first priority to separating aircraft and issuing required safety alerts. Other duties are prioritized by judgment based on the situation.

choices.text: Providing requested additional services before separation tasks.
Completing coordination calls before issuing safety alerts.
Handling aircraft strictly by order of initial call-up.
Separating aircraft and issuing safety alerts as required.

items.question_text: When workload, communications, and equipment permit, which procedural preference applies?
items.explanation: The paragraph prefers automation over nonautomation procedures when feasible, closed-loop automation over open-loop clearances when workload permits, and radar separation over nonradar when operationally advantageous.

choices.text: Use nonradar separation in preference to radar separation whenever both are available.
Avoid closed-loop automation because open-loop clearances are always safer.
Use automation procedures in preference to nonautomation procedures when feasible.
Use automation only when nonautomation procedures are unavailable.

items.question_text: How should controllers generally provide ATC service, unless a listed priority or safety need changes the order?
items.explanation: Operational priority is generally first come, first served as circumstances permit, but safety and the listed priority categories can require different handling.

choices.text: Always in order of aircraft size, with heavy aircraft first.
Always by flight rules, with IFR before VFR in every case.
Only by scheduled arrival time, regardless of operational priority.
On a first-come, first-served basis as circumstances permit.

items.question_text: What is the difference between using 'immediately' and 'expedite' in control instructions?
items.explanation: 'Immediately' is reserved for expeditious compliance needed to avoid an imminent situation. 'Expedite' is for prompt compliance needed to avoid the development of an imminent situation.

choices.text: 'Immediately' addresses an imminent situation; 'expedite' addresses avoiding development of one.
'Expedite' is stronger than 'immediately' and is used only after a missed clearance.
Both words are interchangeable whenever a controller wants faster compliance.
Both words are prohibited unless the pilot declares an emergency.

items.question_text: When must a controller issue a safety alert?
items.explanation: A safety alert is required when the controller is aware an aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft. The controller must not assume another controller has already observed it.

choices.text: Only after the pilot requests a safety alert.
When aware an aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.
Only when the aircraft is operating IFR.
Only if the aircraft is already under the controller's separation responsibility.

items.question_text: When a pilot reports an inflight equipment malfunction, what should the controller determine?
items.explanation: The controller determines the nature and extent of any special handling desired. The report may affect safety, separation standards, IFR capability, RVSM capability, or the flight's ability to proceed.

choices.text: Only whether the aircraft can maintain its assigned altitude.
Only whether the aircraft wants priority handling.
The nature and extent of any special handling desired.
The maintenance cause of the malfunction before taking ATC action.

items.question_text: What does a pilot's declaration of 'minimum fuel' require ATC to do?
items.explanation: Minimum fuel is not an emergency declaration, but it means the pilot cannot accept undue delay. ATC must inform any facility receiving control jurisdiction and remain alert for delays.

choices.text: Treat the aircraft as an emergency aircraft in all cases.
Assign priority over all other aircraft regardless of safety or traffic flow.
Ignore the report unless the pilot declares emergency fuel.
Inform any receiving facility of the minimum fuel problem and be alert for delays.

items.question_text: What kinds of information must be reported as essential flight information?
items.explanation: Controllers must report information about NAS components or flight conditions that may adversely affect air safety to the appropriate facility or office.

choices.text: Information about NAS components or flight conditions that may adversely affect air safety.
Only pilot reports that already qualify as emergencies.
Only information about runway closures at the controller's own airport.
Only weather reports received from certified weather observers.

items.question_text: After one aircraft reports a ground-based NAVAID malfunction, what is the first required ATC action?
items.explanation: The rule begins by requesting a report from a second aircraft. That second report determines whether use can continue, whether the first aircraft must be informed, or whether the NAVAID should be marked unusable and reported.

choices.text: Immediately stop using the NAVAID for all aircraft.
Request a report from a second aircraft.
Assume the report is an airborne receiver malfunction.
Wait for Technical Operations before taking any ATC action.

items.question_text: Who invokes MARSA, and under what kind of operation may it be applied?
items.explanation: MARSA is a military command prerogative, not an individual pilot/unit convenience, and may only be applied to specified military IFR operations documented in an LOA or other appropriate document.

choices.text: Any military pilot may invoke MARSA whenever operating VFR.
ATC invokes MARSA whenever two military aircraft are near each other.
Military command invokes MARSA for specified military IFR operations documented in an appropriate agreement or document.
MARSA may be applied to civil formation flights when workload is high.

items.question_text: When must military additions, modifications, or exceptions to FAA procedures be applied?
items.explanation: Military procedures are prescribed when a common procedure has not been attained or to meet a specific requirement, and they apply at the military facilities or situations specified by the paragraph.

choices.text: Whenever a military aircraft calls any FAA facility.
Only after the pilot requests use of service-specific phraseology.
Never; FAA procedures always supersede military facility procedures.
When prescribed as additions, modifications, or exceptions for the applicable military facility or requirement.

items.question_text: How must ATC treat a formation flight for control purposes?
items.explanation: A formation flight is controlled as a single aircraft. Separation between aircraft within the formation is the responsibility of the flight leader and the other pilots, including join-up and breakaway transition periods.

choices.text: Control the formation as a single aircraft.
Provide standard ATC separation between every aircraft in the formation at all times.
Control each aircraft individually until the flight leader requests otherwise.
Treat the formation as MARSA even if no military command procedure applies.

items.question_text: Before allowing an aircraft under your control to enter another controller's jurisdiction, what must be ensured?
items.explanation: The controller must ensure necessary coordination has been accomplished before allowing an aircraft to enter another controller's area of jurisdiction.

choices.text: The pilot has switched to the next frequency.
Necessary coordination has been accomplished.
The aircraft has crossed the boundary already.
The receiving controller has issued a new clearance directly to the pilot.

items.question_text: Which condition can establish transfer of control of an aircraft?
items.explanation: Control may be transferred at a prescribed or coordinated location, time, fix, or altitude; or after radar handoff and frequency change when authorized by directive or LOA specifying the transfer.

choices.text: Only when the aircraft acknowledges the next controller's first instruction.
Only when the aircraft crosses the facility boundary.
At a prescribed or coordinated location, time, fix, or altitude.
Whenever the transferring controller stops transmitting to the aircraft.

items.question_text: Before issuing a clearance that requires flight within a surface area assigned to a nonapproach control tower, what must ATC normally do?
items.explanation: The rule requires coordination with the appropriate nonapproach control tower on an individual aircraft basis unless otherwise specified in an LOA.

choices.text: Issue the clearance without coordination because approach control owns all surface areas.
Coordinate only when the aircraft is IFR.
Require the pilot to obtain tower approval directly before the clearance is issued.
Coordinate with the appropriate tower on an individual aircraft basis unless an LOA provides otherwise.

items.question_text: When should radio communications normally be transferred to a receiving controller?
items.explanation: Radio communications should be transferred before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area of jurisdiction unless otherwise coordinated or specified by an LOA or facility directive.

choices.text: Before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's jurisdiction, unless otherwise coordinated or specified.
Only after the aircraft has crossed the sector boundary.
Only when the pilot requests a frequency change.
Only after the receiving controller issues a clearance.

items.question_text: How may a controller approve another controller's, pilot's, or vehicle operator's request without a lengthy readback?
items.explanation: The paragraph permits restating the request in complete or abbreviated terms followed by APPROVED, and allows APPROVED AS REQUESTED in lieu of a lengthy readback.

choices.text: Use 'ROGER' because all operational requests are self-approving.
Use 'APPROVED AS REQUESTED' when it can substitute for a lengthy readback.
Use 'WILCO' as the required approval phrase.
Approve only by repeating every word of the request exactly.

items.question_text: When must wake turbulence procedures be applied?
items.explanation: Wake turbulence procedures apply to an aircraft operating behind another aircraft when wake turbulence separation is required.

choices.text: Only when both aircraft are heavy.
Only when the pilot requests wake turbulence separation.
When an aircraft is operating behind another aircraft and wake turbulence separation is required.
Only to VFR aircraft not receiving radar service.

items.question_text: What information should be included in a wake turbulence cautionary advisory when issued?
items.explanation: Wake turbulence cautionary advisories include the position, altitude if known, and direction of flight of the aircraft creating the wake hazard.

choices.text: Position, altitude if known, and direction of flight.
Only aircraft type and speed.
Only the runway and expected departure time.
Only the phrase 'caution wake turbulence' with no traffic information.

items.question_text: When should traffic advisories be issued to aircraft on your frequency?
items.explanation: Unless operating in Class A or the pilot requests omission, issue advisories when, in your judgment, proximity may diminish to less than applicable separation minima. Where no minima apply, issue advisories when proximity warrants.

choices.text: Only after both pilots report each other in sight.
When, in your judgment, proximity may diminish to less than applicable separation minima, unless an exception applies.
Only for IFR-to-IFR traffic in controlled airspace.
Never for VFR aircraft outside Class B or Class C airspace.

items.question_text: What information should known UAS activity advisories include when available?
items.explanation: Known UAS activity advisories should include position, distance, course, type of unmanned aircraft, and altitude when known.

choices.text: Only the operator's name and certificate number.
Only the launch site and recovery site.
Position, distance, course, UA type, and altitude when known.
Only the phrase 'UAS activity' without position or altitude details.

items.question_text: How long must bird activity advisory information continue after receipt unless later information shows it is no longer a factor?
items.explanation: Bird activity advisory information is issued for at least 15 minutes after receipt unless visual observation or later reports show the activity is no longer a factor.

choices.text: Only until the next aircraft lands.
At least 5 minutes.
Until the end of the controller's shift.
At least 15 minutes.

items.question_text: When it appears a pilot's actions constitute a pilot deviation, what notification should be given workload permitting?
items.explanation: The Brasher notification advises the pilot of a possible pilot deviation and provides the facility telephone number for contact, workload permitting.

choices.text: Advise the pilot of a possible pilot deviation and provide the facility telephone number.
Declare an emergency and vector the aircraft to the nearest airport.
Wait until after landing and never advise the pilot on frequency.
Issue a traffic advisory instead of a deviation notification.

items.question_text: What altitude stratum defines RVSM airspace for controller responsibilities?
items.explanation: RVSM airspace is defined as airspace between FL 290 and FL 410 inclusive, where eligible aircraft are separated vertically by 1,000 feet.

choices.text: FL 180 through FL 290 inclusive.
FL 290 through FL 410 inclusive.
At and below FL 180.
Above FL 410 only.

items.situation: A pilot says, 'minimum fuel,' and the aircraft will be handed off to another facility in the next few minutes.
items.instruction: Choose the controller action that fits the minimum-fuel rule.
items.explanation: Minimum fuel is not automatically an emergency, but the next facility must be told and the controller must remain alert for delays that could affect the aircraft.

choices.text: Treat the aircraft as emergency fuel regardless of further pilot statement.
Take no action because minimum fuel has no ATC meaning.
Inform the receiving facility of the minimum fuel problem and watch for delay-causing events.
Tell the pilot to declare an emergency before ATC can consider fuel status.

items.situation: One aircraft reports a ground-based NAVAID malfunction. A second aircraft reports the NAVAID is normal.
items.instruction: What is the appropriate next step?
items.explanation: If the second aircraft reports normal operations, the controller continues use, informs the first aircraft, and records the incident.

choices.text: Immediately declare the NAVAID unusable for all aircraft.
Continue use, inform the first aircraft, and record the incident.
Ignore the first report and make no record.
Stop using the NAVAID until three aircraft report normal operation.

items.situation: A flight of three aircraft is operating as a formation during join-up. A trainee expects ATC to separate each aircraft within the formation.
items.instruction: Who is responsible for separation within the formation?
items.explanation: ATC controls the formation as a single aircraft. Separation within the formation rests with the flight leader and the pilots in the flight, including join-up and breakaway transition periods.

choices.text: ATC, using standard separation between each aircraft in the formation.
Only the lead aircraft's dispatcher.
The flight leader and pilots within the formation.
The receiving controller after handoff.

items.situation: An aircraft is approaching the boundary of the next controller's area. No LOA or coordination allows delayed frequency transfer.
items.para_context: Radio communications should normally be transferred before an aircraft enters the receiving controller's area unless otherwise coordinated or specified by agreement.
items.instruction: When should radio communications be transferred?
items.explanation: Communications should be transferred before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area unless otherwise coordinated or specified.

choices.text: Only after the aircraft crosses the boundary.
Only after the pilot requests the next frequency.
After the receiving controller issues the first control instruction.
Before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area.

items.situation: Two VFR aircraft outside Class B, Class C, and TRSA airspace are on your frequency. No separation minima applies, but their proximity appears likely to become a factor.
items.explanation: Where no separation minima applies, controllers issue traffic advisories to aircraft on their frequency when, in their judgment, proximity warrants.

choices.text: Issue traffic advisories because proximity warrants it.
Withhold traffic advisories because no separation minima applies.
Issue advisories only if both aircraft request them.
Issue advisories only after the aircraft enter Class A airspace.

items.situation: Workload permits and it appears a pilot's actions constitute a pilot deviation.
items.explanation: The required Brasher-style notification advises of a possible pilot deviation and gives the facility telephone number.

choices.text: (Identification) TRAFFIC ALERT ADVISE YOU CONTACT CENTER AFTER LANDING.
(Identification) POSSIBLE PILOT DEVIATION ADVISE YOU CONTACT (facility) AT (telephone number).
(Identification) EMERGENCY DECLARED FOR PILOT DEVIATION.
(Identification) DEVIATION CONFIRMED, CONTACT LAW ENFORCEMENT.
